name: Albino Redwood
primary_sources: Davis, D. F.; Holderman, D. F. (1980). The white redwoods: ghosts of the forest. Naturegraph Publishers.

visual_attributes.morphology: tree with a tall, slender trunk and needle-like leaves
visual_attributes.texture: smooth bark with soft, needle-like leaves
visual_attributes.structural_arrangement: can be basal or aerial, with needles arranged in clusters

domain_specific_visual.phenotype_variation: white, bright yellow, cellular virescent green, pale green, mottled, nonchimeric variegated

functional_attributes.primary_function: survival through parasitic relationship with normal redwoods
secondary_functions: potential ecological role in heavy metal detoxification

domain_specific_functional.metal_accumulation: accumulates more metals due to defective stomata

relationships.description: Albino redwoods graft their roots to normal redwoods to obtain nutrients.
Chimeric redwoods have a mosaic of albino and normal tissues.

symbolic_items: cleansing ceremonies of the Pomo people

contextual_attributes.geographic_range: Henry Cowell Redwoods State Park, Humboldt Redwoods State Park, San Francisco Botanical Garden, Huddart Park, The Santa Lucia Preserve
contextual_attributes.cultural_significance: important in Native American legends and cleansing ceremonies
primary_sources: Davis, D. F.; Holderman, D. F. (1980). The white redwoods: ghosts of the forest. Naturegraph Publishers.

contrastive_constraints: NOT a tree with full chlorophyll production
